session.title: Grounded Retrieval for Offline Lecture Assistants
session.description: An applied lecture on designing audience-side assistants that stay grounded in offline lecture content.

materials.title: Lecture Slides: Grounded Retrieval
materials.contentText: Slides covering grounded retrieval, source priority, unsupported handling, and offline system design.

chunks.heading: Why grounding matters
chunks.text: Grounding constrains answers to verified lecture sources. It reduces hallucination because every answer must be justified by local evidence.

chunks.heading: Source priority
chunks.text: When evidence overlaps, the app should prioritize glossary terms first, lecture materials second, and transcript excerpts third.

chunks.heading: Unsupported questions
chunks.text: If no local evidence supports the question, the system must return a clear unsupported state instead of guessing or improvising.

materials.title: Handout: Offline Architecture Checklist
materials.contentText: A concise handout covering the on-device architecture for mobile lecture assistants.

chunks.heading: Offline storage
chunks.text: SQLite on device stores lecture sessions, materials, chunks, transcript entries, summaries, notes, bookmarks, and question-answer history.

chunks.heading: Retrieval pipeline
chunks.text: A local retrieval pipeline should tokenize the question, score glossary and content matches, and only pass ranked lecture evidence into the answering step.

chunks.heading: Exam focus
chunks.text: For assessment prep, students should remember the grounding rule, the source-priority order, and the unsupported-question behavior.

glossary.definition: Grounding is the rule that an answer must be supported by the lecture pack's local evidence rather than outside knowledge.
Chunking splits lecture material into smaller searchable passages so retrieval can rank the most relevant evidence.
Source priority defines which lecture sources should be preferred first: glossary, then lecture materials, then transcript.
An unsupported question is a question with insufficient local lecture evidence, so the system must clearly decline to answer.

transcript.text: Our audience app is not a free-form chatbot. It behaves more like a constrained assistant over the lecture pack on the device.
If the glossary defines the concept directly, use that before you lean on slides or transcript excerpts.
Students should see an unsupported state when the evidence is missing, because pretending to know the answer is worse than saying not supported.
Everything important for the session lives locally: notes, bookmarks, question history, and all the source content needed for retrieval.

summaries.title: Lecture overview
summaries.text: The lecture defines grounded answering for offline lecture assistants, explains ranked local retrieval, and emphasizes explicit unsupported handling.

summaries.title: Exam focus
summaries.text: Remember the glossary > materials > transcript ordering, the reason grounding matters, and the rule for unsupported questions.

qaCategories.label: Concepts
qaCategories.description: Definitions, terminology, and core lecture concepts.

qaCategories.label: Applications
qaCategories.description: How the lecture ideas apply in a real system.

qaCategories.label: Logistics
qaCategories.description: Schedule, room, or session flow questions.

publicQa.questionText: Why does the app check glossary evidence before transcript snippets?

answer.text: The app checks glossary evidence first because source priority says glossary definitions are the most authoritative local source, ahead of lecture materials and transcript excerpts.

publicQa.questionText: What data is kept on the device for the lecture assistant to work offline?

answer.text: The offline app keeps lecture sessions, materials, chunks, transcript entries, summaries, notes, bookmarks, and question-answer history in on-device SQLite storage.

publicQa.questionText: What should the system do with unsupported questions?

answer.text: Unsupported questions must return a clear unsupported state. The system should not guess, improvise, or behave like a free-form chatbot.
